learning_outcomes.description: Recognise the contribution of key Australian screen works to national and international storytelling; contextualise these works within their original time and place; and evaluate them through different critical lenses
Explain how Australian screen works have been shaped over time by intersections of local and international cultural movements, history, geography, economics, politics, industry, and technology
Demonstrate an awareness of the longstanding and ongoing contributions of Indigenous and other Australians to screen storytelling, cultural policy, and criticism
Have experience communicating individual encounters with screen texts through discussion and reflection to understand engagement with media in social and historical context
Apply skills in locating, contextualising, analysing, and evaluating primary sources, and have experience synthesising this analysis into clear, evidence-based written arguments.

Apply skills in close viewing and listening to identify significant formal and stylistic elements in screen works, and experience describing these elements clearly to support arguments in written or video assignments.

assessments.weighting: <dd>30% 10 weeks, 5 marks per week, best 6 workshop exercises count for the final mark </dd>
assessments.task_description:
30% in-class writing for workshops:

From Week 2, there will be a timed writing exercise that must be completed on the provided worksheet. You are responsible for bringing your own pen or pencil. Students are not permitted to access devices of any kind during the timed task, but may consult printed or handwritten notes. The exercises will be different each week. From weeks 2-6 the exercises will be concerned with developing the skills you will need to complete your historical film reception case study. This gives you the option to complete part of the work for the film reception case study in your workshops and receive marks for that work and informal group feedback as you progress. The exercises from weeks 7-12 will be developing the skills for your final assignment due in Week 13.

Feedback will be given informally. Tutors will collect the worksheets afterwards to record completion of the assessment task. There are 10 opportunities to complete the task throughout the semester. Each will be marked out of 5. Only your best 6 workshop exercises will count towards your final mark out of 30. To check this grade during the semester, please ask your tutor. Because only 6 of the 10 count, there is flexibility to miss up to 4 sessions (due to illness or any other reason) with no penalty and no documentation required.

If you have a disability support plan relevant to the task in place, please contact the course coordinator as soon as possible. Reasonable accommodations can be made for matters documented in a disability support plan (for example, you are unable to hand write under timed conditions).

The 4-session buffer described above is intended to cover ordinary absences. A separate make-up session is available only to students who, for a genuine and documented reason, miss more than 4 of the exercises (for example, a sudden or serious illness, or another documented cause requiring a lengthy absence). Missing 4 or fewer sessions is already covered by the best-6-of-10 structure and does not qualify for a make-up. If you miss more than 4 sessions due to a documented reason, please contact the course coordinator to make alternative arrangements.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
A list of films and TV programs relevant to the first half of the course will be provided on Blackboard. Choose ONE from the list and perform a historical reception study and reflection following the instructions provided. Note, as part of your submission you will need to provide screenshots and links to specific historical writing on your chosen film or TV show that you locate in Trove, Proquest Historical Newspapers, or Media History Lantern. The workshop exercises in weeks 2-6 will give you the opportunity to develop and practice or do some of the actual work for your historical reception case study. You will be allowed to include (and build upon) that in-class work in this assignment if you choose.

Please see Blackboard for detailed instructions.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This task asks you to take on the role of a film festival curator organising a double bill for the opening night of a festival titled “Australia: Then and Now.” You will choose two film and/or TV texts (including one from the course screening list) for your double bill and prepare a web resource using Adobe Express that would help guide film festival goers in understanding the screening and your rationale for the double bill.

Your reflective comparative essay will centre your position as an expert, informed viewer of Australian film and television through close comparative analysis of your chosen texts and with support from set course readings. You will make a connection to at least one of the weekly topics from the course through your interpretation of the theme “Australia: Then and Now”, and invite your imagined festival audience to re-evaluate the two texts in the double bill through the lens of your comparative analysis.

Support for using Adobe Express and developing your reflective essay will be provided in workshops. 

For marking criteria and further information, please see Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit via TurnItIn on Blackboard. The full essay including references and AI use disclosure will be submitted to TurnItIn as a Word document or PDF and include a link to your Adobe Express website.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1 Seminar: Situating Australian Film and TV

This first seminar gives an overview of the course the assessment and expectations for the semester, and we start to think about the different factors that have shaped and continue to shape Australian Film and TV in its different forms, such as language, population, economics, politics, location, post-colonialism, genre, critical frameworks. To prep for class, please go to Blackboard and access the required materials in the learning resources folder for Week 1.

Viewing: The Castle (Sitch, 1997)


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

Week 1 Workshop

As the first major assignment is a historical film reception study, you will need to learn how to find historical reception evidence (such as reviews) in the online archives. This workshop helps you start. Before class, please check out the archive links in the Week 1 learning materials folder on Blackboard.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 Seminar: Australian Silent Cinema

In this week we consider the changing place of Australian storytelling amid the successive reinventions of the cinema over the silent period. As the institutional form of cinema changed so too did the kinds of production able to be undertaken. We consider Australian cinema's close relation with theatre and live performance and how the circumstances of exhibition and distribution of the silent film initially enabled but later diminished opportunities for Australian story-telling through film. To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.

Viewing: The Sentimental Bloke (Longford, 1919)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 Workshop

This week we will search Trove for sources from the 1900s-1920s about Australian films and filmmaking. What kinds of reception, commentary and reporting about Australian films appear at this time in newspapers, magazines, and other sources?

We start the in-class writing exercises this week. Each is marked out of 5. See the assessment section of Blackboard for more information.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3 Seminar: The Sound Cinema before Television

Cinema reinvented itself with the coming of sound, but it also made feature film production much more expensive and difficult to film outdoors, leading Hollywood to turn to controlled studio filming for several decades. What impact did this have on Australian filmmaking? We look at how technology and film funding policies set in train a limited commercial cinema, and a 16mm non-commercial newsreel documentary filmmaking strand, which was highly regarded internationally. To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.

Viewing: Jedda (Chauvel, 1955), and/or The Back of Beyond (Heyer, 1954)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 Seminar: The Emergence of TV in Australia

Why did Australia get TV so late (1956) compared to the US and the UK (1930s)? Why does Australia have a mix of commercial TV (9, 7, 10) and publicly funded TV (The ABC)? What kind of TV and TV careers has this mix made possible? We will discuss these questions and more. To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.

Viewing: assorted choices from 1970s Australian TV such as Count Down, Number 96, The Aunty Jack Show.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week. learning_activities.topic:
Week 5 Seminar: The Australian New Wave in the 1970s

We look this week at how the emergence of film societies and festivals in Australia in the 1950s, and the rise of television set in train calls for a revival of Australian cinema. Governments in the 1960s and 1970s heeded the call, setting up The Australian Film, Television, and Radio School, and significant funding mechanisms which fostered an explosion of Australian storytelling on the big screen. What kinds of films emerged and what kinds of films were discouraged? How were they received, valued and denigrated? What is the legacy of the Australian New Wave? To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.

Viewing: Picnic at Hanging Rock (Weir, 1975), and/or Wake in Fright (Kotcheff, 1971), and/or My Survival as an Aboriginal (Coffey, 1978).

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6 Seminar: 1980s Commercial Film & TV in Australia

The 1980s was a massive boom for mainstream Australian film-making and big budgeted commercial TV mini-series. Why? We look at the international film market and national cultural policy, broadcasting, and exhibition circumstances that fostered this boom, and the kinds of mythic Australian storytelling that were popularised at this time. Watch Mad Max 2, but also the clips from miniseries in the Week 6 learning materials folder and do the readings. To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.

Viewing: Mad Max 2 (Miller, 1981), and/or Evil Angels (Schepisi, 1988)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7 Seminar: Multicultural and Cross-cultural Encounters

The migrant figure and the cross-cultural encounter in Australian film and TV is not new. We see it some of the films of the New Wave, and in stage and TV comedy of the 1980s. The 1990s saw a particular rise of different kinds of cultural voices in screen story telling. We look at some reasons why and discuss such things as the role of SBS as a broadcaster, shifting cultural policies, diasporic cinema in film festivals, and waves of migration. To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.

Watch: Head On (Kokkinos, 1998, R18+), and/or The Adventures of Priscilla Queen of the Desert (Elliott, 1994), and/or Floating Life (Law, 1996)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8 Seminar: Towards an Indigenous Screen

Indigenous Australians have been recorded and used in screen storytelling by filmmakers since the beginning of cinema in Australia, with sometimes devastating results. In this seminar we look at how protocols, and collaborative and self-representational practices have emerged, what they have looked like at different times, how they've been fostered institutionally, and their significance. To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.

Viewing: Journey Home, David Gulpilil (Miles and Morton-Thomas, 2025), and/or In My Blood it Runs (Newell, 2019)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9 Seminar: Australia and Global Hollywood

How did the Gold Coast of SE QLD become a location for international screen production? How do Australian screen personnel, Australian-based digital visual effects houses, and Australian filming locations serve international screen production? Which Australian actors get to play other nationalities, and which don't, and why? What impact has this international dimension to the Australian screen had on local screen stories and their aesthetics? We will be looking at these questions and more. To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.


Viewing: The Fall Guy (Leitch, 2024), and/or Lion (Davis, 2016)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 Seminar: Australian Genre Cinema

This week we examine Australian contributions to particular transnational film genres in the 2000s with a focus on horror and thrillers, looking at how landscape, Indigenous issues, cross-cultural encounters, and the Australian gothic weave through. How do we explain the international popularity/resonance of such genres, and in what kinds of ways do 2010s and 2020s films differ from those of the Australian genre boom in the 1970s and 1980s? To prep, please read/watch the required viewing/reading in the learning materials folder on Blackboard for this week.


Viewing: The Nightingale (Kent, 2018) and/or Talk to Me (Michael and Danny Philippou, 2022)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 Seminar: The Australian Screen Industry Now

To wrap up, we look at the current state of the Australian film and television landscape. How has digital streaming disrupted Australian film and TV production and what opportunities does it offer for Australian content on the global stage? How are industry professionals navigating the challenges of the current state of the Australian film & TV industry? What does Australian film & TV content look like now, and what are some potential futures for the industry?

To prepare, please see Blackboard for this week's viewing/reading.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06
